Jesus leaves the temple and makes a shocking pronouncement: not one stone will remain standing. His disciples, hearing this, draw Him aside with a question that seems natural enough—when will this happen, and what will signal His return and the end of the age? But Jesus does not indulge their curiosity about dates and timelines. Instead, He does something far more important: He warns them about deception. The warning comes first and comes repeatedly. Before telling them anything about the future, Jesus tells them to see to it that no one deceives them. This is the true danger of the age—not tribulation itself, but the lie that will accompany it. False messiahs will arise claiming to be Christ. False prophets will perform great signs and wonders. The very elect could be deceived if that were possible. Jesus is telling His disciples that in the days leading to His return, the primary spiritual battle will not be against external persecution but against the seduction of lies. The world will grow darker, persecution will come, love will grow cold, but the sharpest weapon wielded against God's people will be deception dressed up as truth.

What Jesus describes next is not a comfortable vision. Wars, famines, earthquakes, persecution, betrayal, and a tribulation so severe that if those days were not cut short, no one would survive—this is the landscape of the age. Yet Jesus tells His disciples not to be alarmed. These things must happen. They are birth pains, not the final death. The gospel will be preached to all nations before the end comes. God's purposes will advance even through chaos. Even in judgment, there is mercy—the days are shortened for the sake of the elect. This tension holds throughout Matthew 24: real suffering, real danger, real deception, but also real purpose. God is not wringing His hands. The future is not out of His control. What appears to be chaos is moving toward a predetermined end, and that end belongs to Christ.

But then Jesus shifts to the most uncomfortable demand of all. No one knows the day or hour. Not the angels. Not even the Son in the days of His flesh. Only the Father knows. And this unknowability is not incidental—it is the entire point. Because you do not know when He will return, you must keep watch. You must be ready. The parables that follow are not gentle invitations to spiritual awareness. They are confrontations with the reality of judgment. The faithful servant who tends his master's household will be exalted. The wicked servant who grows comfortable, who beats his fellow servants, who lives as though the master will not return, will be cut to pieces and assigned a place with the hypocrites, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth. This is not metaphorical language softened for comfort. Jesus is describing hell. The point is razor-sharp: your readiness for His return determines your eternal destiny. You cannot live as though the return of Christ is irrelevant to how you treat others, how you use your time, or where you place your hope. The end is coming. You do not know when. Therefore, live now as though you will give account.
